edges.description: E-cadherin-mediated adherence anchors spores at the epithelial surface, positioning them for access to germinants and nutrients
Depleted microbiota cannot compete with C. difficile

edges.target: Loss of Secondary Bile Acid Production

edges.description: Without competitive exclusion, C. diff spores germinate and vegetative cells proliferate
Elevated primary bile acids and depleted secondary bile acids favor spore germination
edges.source: Loss of Secondary Bile Acid Production

edges.description: Insufficient bile-acid buffering increases effective TcdB intoxication at the mucosa
edges.target: Epithelial Cell Death and Barrier Disruption

edges.description: Vegetative C. difficile produces toxins A and B during stationary phase
Toxins cause cytoskeletal collapse and cell death
edges.target: Epithelial Cell Death and Barrier Disruption

edges.description: Toxins trigger IL-8 release and massive neutrophil influx
CDT in toxin-positive strains further amplifies inflammatory injury programs
Epithelial destruction with inflammatory exudate forms pseudomembranes

edges.description: CDT-driven inflammasome signaling amplifies cytokine-mediated inflammatory recruitment
Neutrophils form the inflammatory core of pseudomembranes

nodes.description: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, cephalosporins) cause indiscriminate killing of gut commensals, dramatically reducing microbial diversity and biomass. This creates ecological opportunity for C. difficile colonization.

evidence.snippet: The most significant risk factor for C difficile infection is broad-spectrum antibiotics. Patients can be colonized with C difficile without symptoms, but antibiotic use disturbs the balance of gut flora, enabling C difficile overgrowth and infection.

nodes.description: Intestinal bile acids can directly buffer TcdB cytotoxicity. When bile-acid buffering is reduced or toxin burden is high, this protection is overcome and toxin-mediated epithelial injury proceeds.

evidence.reference_title: Intestinal bile acids provide a surmountable barrier against C. difficile TcdB-induced disease pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Small molecules extracted from the lumenal contents of the small intestine, cecum, colon, and feces were found to inhibit TcdB in accordance with the differential amounts of total bile acids in each compartment.

nodes.description: In CDT-positive strains, the binary toxin axis can activate inflammasome-linked inflammatory pathways in myeloid cells and increase early disease severity.

evidence.reference_title: Purified CDT toxins and a clean deletion within the CDT locus provide novel insights into the contribution of binary toxin in cellular inflammation and Clostridioides difficile infection.
evidence.snippet: Besides producing the main virulence factors, toxin A (TcdA) and toxin B (TcdB), many of the common clinical strains encode the C. difficile transferase (CDT) binary toxin.

nodes.description: C. difficile spores germinate in the favorable environment created by antibiotic disruption: available nutrients (especially proline), primary bile acids, and lack of competition. Vegetative cells proliferate to high density, occupying the ecological niche vacated by commensals.

evidence.snippet: Patients can be colonized with C difficile without symptoms, but antibiotic use disturbs the balance of gut flora, enabling C difficile overgrowth and infection.

nodes.description: Clostridioides difficile, a Gram-positive anaerobe, builds its peptidoglycan cell wall via the membrane lipid II intermediate, whose D-Ala-D-Ala terminus is the binding target of the glycopeptide vancomycin — the first-line oral therapy for C. difficile infection. Orally administered vancomycin is not absorbed and reaches high luminal colonic concentrations, where sequestering lipid II blocks cell-wall synthesis and kills the organism at the site of disease.

evidence.snippet: the antimicrobial activity of other vancosamine-modified glycopeptides depends upon both d-Ala-d-Ala stem-terminus recognition (primary binding site) and stem-bridge recognition (secondary binding site).

nodes.description: C. difficile spores persist in the gut through adherence to intestinal epithelial cells via interaction with E-cadherin, a transmembrane adhesion molecule on the host epithelium. Proteomic studies have identified four spore surface ligands that bind E-cadherin: the spore coat proteins CotE and CdeM, along with two uncharacterized proteins (CDIF27147_03838 and CDIF27147_02282). These ligands carry discrete E-cadherin-binding motifs (9-20 amino acid regions) that are conserved across all five classical C. difficile clades (C1-C5) and predicted to be surface-exposed. E-cadherin-mediated adherence anchors spores to the epithelial surface, representing a foundational mechanism for spore persistence and recurrent infection. The progression from initial E-cadherin-mediated adherence to stable biofilm formation and recurrent disease remains incompletely understood, particularly regarding the roles of secondary adhesion mechanisms, integration with nutrient/bile acid sensing, and the identity of additional spore-epithelial/spore-spore interactions stabilizing persistent infection.

evidence.reference_title: Coupling Far-Western Blotting with Peptide Microarrays Reveals Novel E-Cadherin Spore-Surface Ligands in Clostridioides difficile.
evidence.snippet: The spore surface proteins CotE (CDIF27147_01458) and CdeM (CDIF27147_01682), along with two uncharacterized proteins, CDIF27147_03838 and CDIF27147_02282, interact with E-cadherin.

evidence.reference_title: Coupling Far-Western Blotting with Peptide Microarrays Reveals Novel E-Cadherin Spore-Surface Ligands in Clostridioides difficile.
evidence.snippet: Comparative genomics showed that these ligands and motifs are conserved across all five classical C. difficile clades (C1-C5).

nodes.description: Toxin-mediated Rho GTPase inactivation causes actin cytoskeleton condensation, loss of cell-cell junctions, and epithelial cell rounding followed by death. This creates gaps in the epithelial barrier, exposing the lamina propria to luminal contents.
nodes.id: Epithelial Cell Death and Barrier Disruption

evidence.reference_title: Clostridium difficile toxins: mechanism of action and role in disease.
evidence.snippet: By glucosylating small GTPases, TcdA and TcdB cause actin condensation and cell rounding, which is followed by death of the cell.

nodes.description: The healthy microbiome normally prevents C. difficile colonization through multiple mechanisms: nutrient competition (especially for proline, amino acids), niche exclusion, and production of bacteriocins and antimicrobial peptides. Antibiotic disruption removes these competitive barriers.

evidence.reference_title: The Roles of Inflammation, Nutrient Availability and the Commensal Microbiota in Enteric Pathogen Infection.
evidence.snippet: A general feature of a normal, healthy gut microbiota can generate conditions in the gut that disfavor colonization of enteric pathogens. This is termed colonization-resistance (CR). Upon disturbance of the microbiota, CR can be transiently disrupted, and pathogens can gain the opportunity to grow to high levels.

evidence.reference_title: Metagenomic analysis reveals distinct patterns of gut microbiota features with diversified functions in C. difficile infection (CDI), asymptomatic carriage and non-CDI diarrhea.
evidence.snippet: The gut microbiota in the Control group showed higher Chao1 index (p < 0.05)... Collinsella_aerofaciens, Collinsella_sp_4_8_47FAA, Collinsella_tanakaei and Collinsella_sp_CAG_166 were enriched in Control.

nodes.description: Loss of bile acid 7α-dehydroxylating bacteria (Clostridium scindens, C. hylemonae, and related taxa) prevents conversion of primary bile acids (cholate, chenodeoxycholate) to secondary bile acids (deoxycholate, lithocholate). Primary bile acids promote C. difficile spore germination; secondary bile acids inhibit both germination and vegetative growth.
nodes.id: Loss of Secondary Bile Acid Production

evidence.reference_title: Strain-Dependent Inhibition of Clostridioides difficile by Commensal Clostridia Carrying the Bile Acid-Inducible (bai) Operon.
evidence.snippet: Inhibition of C. difficile by commensals correlated with the efficient conversion of cholate to deoxycholate, a secondary bile acid that inhibits C. difficile germination, growth, and toxin production.

evidence.reference_title: Functional Intestinal Bile Acid 7α-Dehydroxylation by Clostridium scindens Associated with Protection from Clostridium difficile Infection in a Gnotobiotic Mouse Model.
evidence.snippet: Amendment of Oligo-MM12 with C. scindens normalized the large intestinal bile acid composition by reconstituting 7α-dehydroxylation. These changes had only minor effects on the composition of the native Oligo-MM12, but significantly decreased early large intestinal C. difficile colonization and pathogenesis.

nodes.description: TcdA triggers IL-8 and other chemokine release from epithelial cells, causing massive neutrophil influx into the colonic mucosa. Neutrophils form the characteristic "volcano lesions" and contribute to tissue damage through degranulation, reactive oxygen species, and NET formation.

evidence.reference_title: Clostridium difficile toxins: mechanism of action and role in disease.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, there have been major advances in defining the role of these toxins in modulating the inflammatory events involving the disruption of cell junctions, neuronal activation, cytokine production, and infiltration by polymorphonuclear cells.

nodes.description: Characteristic raised yellow-white plaques on colonic mucosa composed of fibrin, mucus, neutrophils, and cellular debris overlying areas of epithelial necrosis. Represents severe C. difficile infection with high morbidity. Fluid secretion and diarrhea result from barrier loss and inflammation.

evidence.reference_title: Clostridioides difficile toxins: mechanisms of action and antitoxin therapeutics.
evidence.snippet: These toxins promote inflammation, tissue damage and diarrhoea.

nodes.description: C. difficile secretes two large clostridial glucosylating toxins: TcdA (enterotoxin, 308 kDa) and TcdB (cytotoxin, 270 kDa). Both are glucosyltransferases that inactivate Rho family GTPases (Rho, Rac, Cdc42) by monoglucosylation of a critical threonine residue, disrupting the actin cytoskeleton.

evidence.reference_title: Clostridium difficile toxins: mechanism of action and role in disease.
evidence.snippet: Following expression and release from the bacterium, TcdA and TcdB translocate to the cytosol of target cells and inactivate small GTP-binding proteins, which include Rho, Rac, and Cdc42. Inactivation of these substrates occurs through monoglucosylation of a single reactive threonine.

evidence.reference_title: Clostridioides difficile toxins: mechanisms of action and antitoxin therapeutics.
evidence.snippet: The bacterium produces up to three toxins, which are considered the major virulence factors in C. difficile infection. These toxins promote inflammation, tissue damage and diarrhoea.

nodes.description: Oral vancomycin is first-line treatment for C. difficile infection.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Future Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Effective infection management requires appropriate interpretation of diagnostic tests, as well as the use of vancomycin and fidaxomicin as first-line treatment.
